# UC-17.1.55 · Cisco ISE Profiler Probe Quality and Endpoint-Classification Coverage

> **Criticality:** Medium &middot; **Difficulty:** Advanced &middot; **Pillar:** Security &middot; **Type:** Operations, Configuration &middot; **Wave:** Walk &middot; **Status:** Verified

*ISE figures out what each device is — phone, printer, laptop — using several different methods. We check each method is working; if one is broken, ISE may be guessing wrong about what kind of device just connected.*

---

## Description

Detects probe failures, rate-limits, and low-confidence endpoint classifications across ISE's profiler probe types. When probes are unhealthy, endpoints are mis-classified, leading to wrong authorization profiles.

## Value

Profiler is how ISE knows what kind of device just authenticated — printer, IP phone, IoT sensor, laptop. Wrong classification means wrong authorization profile (and possibly wrong VLAN, wrong dACL). Catching probe degradation preserves classification fidelity.

## Implementation

Confirm *Profiler* category at INFO. Schedule every 30 min, search window `-2h@h to @h`, trigger on count > 0.

## Control test

### Positive scenario

Disable the DHCP probe on a PSN. Within 30 min, classification confidence drops. UC-17.1.55 fires showing `dhcp` probe with `health=degraded`.

### Negative scenario

Re-enable the probe. Health returns to 'healthy'.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- ISE Profiler enabled with multiple probe types active.
- `device_inventory.csv` for cross-reference.

### Step 1 — Configure data collection
Enable *Profiler* category at INFO.

### Step 2 — Create the search
Search in `spl` field. Schedule every 30 min.

### Step 3 — Validate
Provoke as above.

### Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard "ISE — Profiler Probe Health":
- Per-probe health tile.
- Confidence distribution.
- Top-N low-confidence device types.

### Step 5 — Troubleshooting
- **All probes show degraded** — PSN resource saturation; cross-ref UC-17.1.29.
- **DHCP probe absent** — DHCP relay not configured to forward DHCP to PSN.
- **NMAP probe disabled** — Either by policy (firewall) or PSN config.

## SPL

```spl
(index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (80001, 80003, 80004))
  OR (index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:profiler_feed")
| stats count, dc(endpoint_mac) as unique_endpoints, count(eval(MessageCode=="80003")) as probe_failures, count(eval(MessageCode=="80004")) as rate_limits, avg(confidence_score) as avg_confidence by probe_type
| eval health = case(probe_failures > 0 OR rate_limits > 0, "degraded", avg_confidence < 70, "low_confidence", true(), "healthy")
| where health != "healthy"
```

## Visualization

(1) Per-probe health tile. (2) Confidence score distribution per probe. (3) Top-N: device types with low confidence.

## Known False Positives

- **NMAP probe firewall block** — Some networks block active scanning; tag and exclude.
- **DHCP probe coverage gap** — DHCP probe requires SPAN or DHCP relay; coverage may be partial.
- **NetFlow probe rate-limit during traffic peak** — During a traffic spike, NetFlow probe may be rate-limited; expected.

## References

- [Cisco ISE 3.4 — Endpoint Profiling](https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/security/ise/3-4/admin_guide/b_ise_admin_3_4/m_profiling.html)
